skippedSources.reason: Paywall teaser — email body contains only promo text, testimonials, and a link to the paid dashboard. Zero analytical content or data.

skippedSources.reason: Paywall teaser — headline bullets only with no data, numbers, or analytical sentences.

items.title: Retatrutide Phase 3: 28.3% weight loss (70 lbs) — comparable to bariatric surgery, no cardiac issues
items.idea: Eli Lilly's retatrutide, the first triple agonist (GLP-1+GIP+Glucagon), delivered 28.3% body weight loss (70.3 lbs) over 80 weeks in the 2,339-patient TRIUMPH-1 trial vs 2.2% placebo. 65.3% of participants dropped below BMI 30 (obesity cutoff), and 45.3% hit at least 30% weight loss — metrics historically achieved only by bariatric surgery. No cardiac or liver issues observed. TRIUMPH-2 (type 2 diabetes) and TRIUMPH-3 (cardiovascular disease) readouts expected later this year.
items.angle: The 45% who hit ≥30% weight loss is the surgical benchmark. If retatrutide reaches market, every obesity drug gets repriced — including Lilly's own Zepbound. Triple agonism is a step-change from dual agonists.
items.tweetA: Retatrutide Phase 3: 28.3% weight loss vs 2.2% on placebo. 65% of patients dropped below BMI 30. 45% lost 30%+ of body weight — that's the bariatric surgery benchmark. A triple-agonist pill just crossed the surgical threshold. The obesity war just escalated. $LLY
items.tweetB: The GLP-1 story keeps compounding. Retatrutide adds glucagon activation to GLP-1+GIP, which increases resting energy expenditure on top of appetite suppression. 70 lbs average loss. No cardiac issues. The next question: does this cannibalize Zepbound or expand the addressable market? $LLY

items.title: US adult obesity peaked in 2021 — first sustained decline in modern records; GLP-1 prescriptions 5.8% to 12.4% of US adults
items.idea: Epic Research's Cosmos dataset (300M+ US patient records) shows adult obesity peaked in 2021 and has trended down — the first sustained decline in modern records. GLP-1 prescriptions roughly doubled from 5.8% of US adults in early 2024 to 12.4% by 2025. 1-in-8 American adults on a metabolic drug at the same time obesity starts structurally declining is a consumer behavior signal of historic scale.
items.angle: If 12.4% of US adults are on GLP-1s and obesity is declining for the first time ever, second-order effects are enormous: food reformulation, bariatric surgery volumes, orthopedics, diabetes equipment, life insurance mortality tables.
items.tweetA: US adult obesity peaked in 2021. Declining for the first time in modern medical records. GLP-1 prescriptions hit 12.4% of US adults. If this is causal, we're running real-world drug trials at population scale — and the entire healthcare cost curve is changing underneath us.
items.tweetB: 1-in-8 American adults is now on a GLP-1. Obesity rates falling for the first time in history. The downstream losers: bariatric surgery, diabetes supply chains, specific food categories. None of this is in consensus estimates for those sectors.

items.title: NextEra + Dominion: $67B AI-era energy merger — even the world's largest green energy builder said its scale was insufficient
items.idea: NextEra agreed to buy Dominion Energy for $67B all-stock, creating the 3rd-largest US energy company ($249B market cap, $420B EV). Combined pipeline: 130 GW of large-load opportunities. Four largest hyperscalers committed $725B in 2026 capex. EPRI projects data centers at 17% of US electricity by 2030. Industry plans $1.1T in new generation and transmission over 5 years. NEE, already world's largest wind/solar producer, concluded its scale was insufficient.
items.angle: When the world's largest green energy builder says 'we need to be bigger,' that is the strongest signal about the AI power demand curve. $1.1T grid capex in 5 years is a number the market consistently underweights.
items.tweetA: NextEra just bought Dominion for $67B. The reason: Northern Virginia — data center capital of the world — needs power NEE's scale couldn't cover. When the world's largest green energy builder says 'we're too small,' that tells you something about what's coming. $NEE $D
items.tweetB: Hyperscalers: $725B capex in 2026. EPRI: data centers = 17% of US electricity by 2030. Grid industry: $1.1T in new infrastructure over 5 years. And yet a $67B merger is still the headline. The power capex math is still being underwritten by the market.

items.title: SpaceX S-1: $75B raise at $1.75T valuation — Starlink profitable, AI burning billions, xAI leases Colossus to Anthropic at $1.25B/month
items.idea: SpaceX S-1 targets $1.75T valuation and $75B raise (largest IPO ever). 2025 revenue $18.7B, net loss $4.94B. Starlink: $11.4B revenue, $4.4B operating income, 10.3M subscribers in 164 countries. AI: $3.2B revenue, $6.4B loss, $12.7B capex. xAI committed full Colossus 1 capacity to Anthropic at $1.25B/month through May 2029 (terminable 90 days notice). Musk holds 85.1% voting power via Class B (10 votes each). Roadshow starts first week of June.
items.angle: The $1.25B/month Anthropic compute contract is $15B/year in contracted AI infrastructure revenue from one customer. Starlink at $4.4B operating income is already a profitable standalone business subsidizing the AI war chest.
items.tweetA: SpaceX S-1: Starlink does $4.4B operating income on 10.3M subscribers. That business alone justifies a serious valuation. The $75B raise funds a $12.7B AI capex budget burning $6.4B/yr. Starlink profits are the AI war chest.
items.tweetB: Buried in the SpaceX S-1: xAI committed Colossus 1 to Anthropic at $1.25B/month through May 2029. That's $15B/year in compute revenue from ONE customer. AI infrastructure is now a long-term contracted business — not a narrative.

items.title: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh signals Greenspan-style approach — deliberate ambiguity as a tool
items.idea: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has suggested he may take an Alan Greenspan-style approach — strategic ambiguity, elaborate constructions that preserve maximum Fed optionality, less forward guidance. Warsh inherits a Fed navigating PCE approaching 4% on war-driven energy, an inelastic economy, and a bond market that has been systematically wrong about inflation in every prior cycle.
items.angle: Greenspan-style ambiguity in a 4% PCE environment means wider distribution of rate outcomes and higher vol for rate-sensitive assets.
items.tweetA: New Fed Chair Warsh is signaling a Greenspan-style approach — deliberate ambiguity, less forward guidance. The last time the Fed operated this way, inflation was not approaching 4%. Constructive ambiguity cuts differently when you're on the wrong side of the breakeven.
items.tweetB: Warsh at the Fed. PCE approaching 4%. Bond market pricing 2.4% 10-year CPI. Either the market is right and Warsh has a smooth ride, or the bond market is wrong — as it has been in every prior cycle — and Warsh inherits the biggest credibility test since Volcker.

items.title: Morgan Stanley 'Inelastic Economy': AI capex doesn't care if borrowing costs 5.5% or 6% — demand not responding to price
items.idea: MS Sunday Start: US upgraded to +2.3% growth in 2026 (from +1.8%) and +2.6% in 2027. Tech firms issuing record bonds at 5.5-6% because for AI buildout, cost of capital is secondary to strategic imperative. US airfares +20.7% YoY. April 2026 gasoline consumption approximately equal to April 2025 despite higher prices. Retail sales ex-oil/gas beat. Inelasticity extends to financing.
items.angle: Inelastic demand breaks traditional monetary transmission. The Fed raises rates and the parts of the economy driving inflation — AI capex, AI-adjacent services, energy — don't respond. It can only cool rate-sensitive segments which are not the inflationary source.
items.tweetA: Morgan Stanley's macro call: the US economy is now 'inelastic.' AI capex doesn't respond to rate changes. Airfares up 21%. Gas demand flat despite higher prices. The Fed raises rates — it just can't slow the parts of the economy that are actually causing the inflation.
items.tweetB: Inelastic demand is the macro regime that rate models weren't built for. When every tech firm views AI as existential, 5.5% vs 6% is noise. MS upgraded US 2026 growth to 2.3%. The economy is fine. The bond market is paying for it.

items.title: Fed's favored PCE gauge rapidly approaching 4% — war-driven energy spike risks broadening into services
items.idea: Bloomberg reports the Fed's top-line PCE gauge is rapidly approaching 4% as war-driven energy cost spikes generate concern that price pressures will broaden. The Persian Gulf situation is the named driver. Apollo's Torsten Slok counters: chip demand remains strong, economy fundamentally fine. Both views can be simultaneously true.
items.angle: PCE at 4% is a credibility crisis for a Fed that anchored at 2%. The key variable: does energy pass through to services and wages, or does it roll off when the conflict ends?
items.tweetA: PCE approaching 4%. The Fed's favorite gauge. The same Fed that spent years anchoring at 2%. We're not in the 'last mile' problem anymore — we're discussing whether a war-driven energy spike becomes a wage-price dynamic. Warsh's first test just got much harder.
items.tweetB: Two views on the same data: Apollo says chips strong, consumer resilient, economy fine. Bloomberg says PCE approaching 4%, war inflation risks broadening. Both can be right simultaneously. That's what makes this cycle so difficult to trade.

items.title: Bond market 'delusional' about inflation in every cycle — currently pricing 2.4% CPI for next 10 years again
items.idea: 10Y TIPS-implied breakeven is 2.40% (10Y yield 4.56% minus TIPS yield 2.16%). Track record: May 2016 — market priced 1.8%, actual averaged 3.2% over 10 years. May 2021 — market priced 2.4%, first 5 years averaged 4.5%. If actual 10-year CPI averages 3.5%, TIPS buyers get ~5.66% combined yield — implying nominal 10Y should trade at 5.66%, not 4.56%. That's 110bps of mispricing.
items.angle: The same 2.4% number that was wrong in May 2021 is being priced in May 2026. War-driven energy, inelastic AI capex, fiscal expansion. If history rhymes, buyers of nominal 10Y at 4.56% are in for the same treatment as the 2021 cohort.
items.tweetA: May 2021: bond market priced 2.4% CPI for 10 years. Actual first 5 years: 4.5% average. May 2026: bond market is pricing... 2.4% CPI for 10 years. The bond market has been systematically delusional about inflation in every single cycle. $TLT
items.tweetB: The 10Y TIPS breakeven is 2.40%. If inflation averages 3.5% over the next decade — conservative given war-driven energy and AI buildout — nominal 10Y should yield 5.66%, not 4.56%. That's 110bps of mispricing baked into the longest part of the curve.

charts.desc: Chart showing TIPS vs 10Y nominal — the delusional gap between market-priced and actual CPI in 2016 and 2021 cycles. Worth opening.

items.title: TIP/TLT ratio at multi-year highs; 7Y has best technical setup into month-end; $69B 2Y auction Tuesday
items.idea: TIP/TLT ratio at very lofty levels comparable to May 2021. $69B new 2-year note auction starts Tuesday, followed by 5s and 7s. Technical: 2Y support 4.20%, 5Y 4.25-4.35%, 7Y 4.40% at trendline with momentum rolling over from oversold. Call: '7Y has the best looking setup' — favors duration out the curve into month-end Friday. Seasonal May tailwinds in play.
items.angle: Buy-the-supply setup into $69B 2Y auction is the classic mean-reversion trade. Wildcard: Iran oil deal could collapse the inflation trade overnight.
items.tweetA: Before Tuesday's $69B 2Y auction: TIP/TLT ratio at multi-year highs. 7Y yield at trendline support. Momentum rolling over from oversold. Seasonal May tailwinds. The setup says buy duration into month-end. Wildcard: an Iran oil deal makes this a momentum trade.
items.tweetB: Three auctions this week: $69B 2Y, then 5s and 7s. BondBeat call: 7Y has the cleanest technical setup — trendline, oversold momentum, seasonal tailwinds into May month-end. If energy headlines cooperate, duration has a real window to fight back.

charts.desc: Three rate charts (2Y/5Y/7Y daily bars) with prior-May seasonal overlays — the 7Y setup is the key actionable chart this week

items.title: Duration bulls waterboarded: MS stopped out of 5Y longs, long bond 'trades like Argentina,' equities ignoring all of it
items.idea: Morgan Stanley stopped out of long 5Y and 7s30s steepener positions. BofA 2Y shorts raising targets higher. Repricing narrative: shifted from 'eventual cuts' to 'surprise — inflation still exists.' The long bond trading 'like Argentina' with convexity hedgers pouring jet fuel on yields. Equities 'ignoring all of this like a golden retriever in a house fire.'
items.angle: The equity-rates divergence is the most obvious macro trade setup right now. One of these two markets is priced wrong. History says equities catch up to rates, not the other way around.
items.tweetA: The long bond is trading like Argentina. MS stopped out of 5Y longs. Convexity hedgers pouring jet fuel on yields. And the equity market? 'Like a golden retriever in a house fire.' This divergence doesn't last. One of these two markets is wrong.
items.tweetB: Duration bulls got mugged. MS out of 5Y longs. BofA bears raising targets. Market repriced from 'eventual cuts' to 'inflation still exists.' Meanwhile equity vol near lows. Rates and equities are telling different stories about the same economy. History says equities catch up to rates.

items.title: Tariff refunds showing up 'in a big way' on the US government's fiscal bottom line
items.idea: Tariff refunds are appearing materially on US government fiscal statements, eroding the net revenue impact. This matters because tariff revenues were supposed to offset fiscal expansion costs. If refunds flow at scale, the bond market gets another data point that the fiscal position is worse than gross tariff numbers imply.
items.angle: Combined pressure on the long end: tariff refunds eroding gross revenues + tech firms record issuance + fiscal expansion = one-way supply pressure. Worth tracking in May/June Treasury monthly statements.
items.tweetA: Tariff refunds showing up 'in a big way' on the US government's books. The trade war was supposed to generate revenues to offset fiscal expansion. It's generating a lot of refund processing instead. The bond market is watching the fiscal trajectory very carefully.
items.tweetB: Add it to the list of long-end pressure: tariff refunds eroding gross revenues, tech firms issuing record bonds, term premium repricing. The 'inelastic economy' is thriving. The bond market is slowly being handed the bill for it.

items.title: More older Americans returning to workforce — out of financial need, not choice
items.idea: A rising trend of older Americans rejoining the labor force is driven by financial necessity rather than preference. Suggests either: (a) the wealth effect hasn't protected the bottom quintile of retirees, or (b) war-driven inflation has eroded real retirement income faster than nominal assets appreciated. Adds to labor supply at the margin but doesn't address the inflationary source.
items.angle: 'Record household wealth' is a median/aggregate illusion. The lower-income retirees living on fixed income crushed by 4% PCE are re-entering the labor market. More labor supply that doesn't fix the actual inflationary source.
items.tweetA: More older Americans going back to work — out of financial need, not choice. Markets at record highs. 'Record household wealth.' But the bottom quintile of retirees is re-entering the labor force to make ends meet. The wealth distribution is being obscured by the averages.
items.tweetB: Participation rate tick-up from older workers = dovish on wages, in theory. Except the inflation driving them back to work is war-driven energy — the thing rates can't fix. More labor supply that doesn't address the actual inflationary source. It's a self-defeating dynamic.

items.title: OpenAI reasoning model disproves Erdős 1946 unit distance conjecture — first AI resolution of a major open math problem
items.idea: An OpenAI internal reasoning model autonomously produced a counterexample to Erdős's 1946 unit distance conjecture — first AI to resolve a prominent open math problem without human mathematical guidance. Construction uses infinite unramified tower of totally real number fields with 3-power Galois groups (Golod-Shafarevich theory + Galois cohomology), producing n^(1.014) unit-distance pairs vs grid's n^1. Verified by nine external mathematicians including Noga Alon, Tim Gowers, and Melanie Matchett Wood.
items.angle: Closing an 80-year-old conjecture using Galois cohomology — tools far from typical training distribution — cannot be dismissed as benchmark gaming. This is the strongest real-world signal yet that frontier reasoning models are doing genuine mathematical research.
items.tweetA: OpenAI's reasoning model just closed an 80-year-old open problem in mathematics. Erdős's unit distance conjecture, 1946. The proof uses infinite towers of totally real number fields — not brute force. Nine external mathematicians including Tim Gowers verified it. This is not a benchmark. This is actual research.
items.tweetB: An AI autonomously applied Galois cohomology and Golod-Shafarevich theory to disprove a conjecture that stumped Erdős for 80 years. We need to update our prior on what 'frontier reasoning' means. The math community now has to figure out how to credit this.

items.title: Code-as-harness survey: next wave of agent performance comes from harness engineering, not base model scale
items.idea: 100+ page survey frames the agent harness as a first-class research object. Code-as-harness consistently outperforms JSON-call orchestration due to determinism, composability, and inspectability. Four properties for production agents: executable, inspectable, stateful, governed. Audits five major open-source frameworks — all make critical architecture decisions implicitly. Core thesis: future agent progress comes from harness-level innovation, not stronger models.
items.angle: If harness quality is the bottleneck, value accrues to whoever invests in harness engineering early. This is also an argument for why Claude Code outperforms raw API calls in production: well-engineered harness around a capable model.
items.tweetA: A 100-page survey makes the case: the next wave of agent performance comes from harness engineering, not bigger models. Code-as-harness beats JSON orchestration on every benchmark. We've been optimizing the wrong layer of the stack.
items.tweetB: 'Executable, inspectable, stateful, governed' — the four properties that separate production agents from demo agents. Five major frameworks audited — all let defaults make critical decisions. Building agents in 2026 without reading this is technical debt.

items.title: NanoGPT-Bench: coding agents recover only 9.3% of human R&D progress — mostly hyperparameter tuning, rarely algorithmic invention
items.idea: Intology drops agents (Claude Code, Codex, Autoresearch) into NanoGPT Speedrun starting from September 2025 world record with 512 H100-hour budget each. Result: under 10% recovery of the next 5 months of human progress. Consistent failure: agents spend budget on hyperparameter tuning rather than algorithmic research. Claude Code and Autoresearch reason about algorithmic changes more often — they just avoid implementing them.
items.angle: Precise calibration for the 'AI is doing AI research' claim. Agents can optimize, they cannot invent. The 90.7% gap between tuning and algorithmic research is where the next 10x comes from — open research problem, not scaling problem.
items.tweetA: NanoGPT-Bench: coding agents burned 512 H100-hours each and recovered 9.3% of human R&D progress. Mostly tuned hyperparameters. Rarely attempted real algorithmic research. The 'AI is doing AI research' narrative is 9.3% real and 90.7% aspiration.
items.tweetB: Current coding agents can optimize. They cannot invent. Claude Code reasons about algorithmic changes — it just doesn't implement them. NanoGPT-Bench gives you the number: 9.3% recovery. The gap between tuning and inventing is where the next frontier actually lives.

items.title: Weak-model critic-comparator: GPT-5.4 nano + k=8 sampling matches Gemini 3 Pro on SWE-bench at nano cost
items.idea: GPT-5.4 nano wrapped in critic-comparator loop (sample k=8, select winner with execution and proof signals) reaches 76.4% SWE-bench Verified — matching standalone Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5 Thinking. Selection is the bottleneck, not the base model. Even with 8x sampling, approach is cheaper because dominant cost is model size, not call count.
items.angle: This breaks the 'buy the most expensive API call' model for coding. Value accrues to whoever builds the best verifier/selector. Orchestration quality is the differentiator, not raw model capability.
items.tweetA: GPT-5.4 nano + k=8 sampling + execution verifier = 76.4% SWE-bench. Same score as Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5 Thinking. The cheapest model is matching the most expensive — because the bottleneck was always selection quality, not generation quality.
items.tweetB: The SWE-bench leaderboard is secretly a verifier quality competition. Sample 8 candidates from a cheap model, pick the best with execution signals: frontier results at nano cost. The model you call matters less than the loop you build around it.

items.title: Meta AIRA: two-agent system beats Llama 3.2 at three scales via agentic neural architecture search in 24 hours
items.idea: Meta's AIRA uses AIRA-Compose (macro architecture search) and AIRA-Design (low-level mechanism implementation) to autonomously discover neural architectures that beat Llama 3.2 at 350M, 1B, and 3B parameter scales within a 24-hour compute budget. Planner/implementer decomposition outperforms single end-to-end agent. Human-led NAS research typically takes months of ablations.
items.angle: If agentic NAS produces competitive architectures in 24 hours, architecture design becomes a commodity faster than expected. The planner-implementer decomposition pattern is portable to almost any complex multi-step task.
items.tweetA: Meta's AIRA: two agents collaborating autonomously discovered neural architectures that beat Llama 3.2 at 350M, 1B, and 3B scales — in 24 hours. Human NAS takes months. If agentic search can replace that, architecture design is the next thing to be automated.
items.tweetB: The planner-implementer decomposition in AIRA is the pattern worth stealing. End-to-end agents fail on complex search problems. Split the task: one composes structure, one implements mechanism. The NAS result is impressive; the architecture pattern is portable to almost everything.

items.title: MeMo: separate trained memory model with explicit interfaces enables continual learning without catastrophic forgetting
items.idea: MeMo augments frozen LLMs with a separately trained memory model (explicit read/write/integrate interfaces). Memory updates decoupled from backbone weights, enabling continual learning without catastrophic forgetting — property RAG cannot deliver. Weekly knowledge updates feasible without retraining. Most production agents still use vector store as memory; MeMo argues memory should be a trained component.
items.angle: If memory is a trained subsystem with interfaces rather than a retrieval hack, long-running agent platforms need to rearchitect. Memory as moat: best memory model for a domain = compound advantage over RAG-based competitors.
items.tweetA: MeMo: memory as a separately trained model with explicit read/write/integrate interfaces. Decoupled from backbone weights. Continual learning without catastrophic forgetting. Weekly knowledge updates without retraining. The 'vector store bolted onto an LLM' era of agent memory has a named alternative.
items.tweetB: The difference between RAG and MeMo: RAG retrieves from a database. MeMo learns. One forgets nothing but can't generalize. The other generalizes and learns continuously. If agents need persistent memory over months, this is the architecture to watch.

items.source: Nous Research via NLP News / Elvis Saravia (nlp.elvissaravia.com)
items.title: Contrastive Neuron Attribution: remove AI behaviors by ablating 0.1% of MLP neurons — no SAE, no retraining, no capability loss
items.idea: Nous Research CNA identifies and ablates top 0.1% of MLP neurons whose activations differ most between contrastive prompt pairs. Removes targeted behavior while leaving model intact. No sparse autoencoder, no weight modification, no benchmark degradation. Validated on refusal circuit across 8 models including Llama-3.1-70B and Qwen2.5-72B. More robust than residual-stream methods at high intervention strengths.
items.angle: Interpretability is now a surgical deployment tool. Enterprise deployments can remove specific behaviors without fine-tuning. The dual-use problem is immediate and obvious: same technique removes harmful and safety behaviors with equal efficiency.
items.tweetA: Nous Research CNA: find the 0.1% of MLP neurons driving a specific behavior. Ablate them. Behavior gone. Model intact. No SAE, no retraining, no capability loss. Interpretability just became a deployment tool — not just a research exercise.
items.tweetB: CNA can surgically remove the refusal circuit from Llama 3.1 70B by ablating 0.1% of neurons. No fine-tuning. No degradation. The safety community has been warning that mechanistic interpretability is dual-use. Here is the precise, reproducible demonstration.

items.title: Crypto's new ideology is hyperfinancialization, not decentralization — three pillars reframe the entire thesis
items.idea: Bankless co-host David sold all his ETH, acknowledging it's time to consider a broader crypto universe. New ideology = hyperfinancialization. Three pillars: (1) Export sovereign financialization as US public good, (2) Decentralize agentic rails for humanity, (3) Promote self-determination. Key distinction: Bitcoin is 'a monetary experiment enabled by technology.' Most of crypto is 'a technology experiment enabled by money.' That distinction is the entire ballgame.
items.angle: Assets aligned with pillars 1 and 3 (stablecoin infrastructure, RWA tokenization, prediction markets) have structural Washington tailwinds post-CLARITY Act. Bitcoin as reserve asset is orthogonal to all three pillars.
items.tweetA: Bankless just sold all his ETH. He says it's time to rethink what crypto represents. The essay names the new ideology: hyperfinancialization. Not decentralization. The thesis: crypto is how America exports its greatest asset — financial might — to the 4B people outside a functioning financial system.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin = monetary experiment enabled by technology. Everything else in crypto = technology experiment enabled by money. That distinction is the entire ballgame. Bitcoin wins when ideology wins. Crypto wins when financialization wins. You can believe in both without contradiction.

items.title: Stablecoins settled $33T in 2025 (>Visa), tokenized RWAs +266% to $31B, US Treasuries on-chain cross $10B
items.idea: Stablecoins settled $33 trillion in 2025, surpassing Visa's annual throughput, 90%+ dollar-denominated. Framed as eurodollarization extending dollar liquidity to 4B+ people outside functioning financial systems. Tokenized RWAs grew 266% in 2025 to $31B on-chain as of mid-2026. Tokenized US Treasuries crossed $10B, led by BlackRock, Ondo, Circle. Global AI infrastructure spending: $700B in Q1 2026 alone. 40% of all crypto VC in 2025 into AI-integrated blockchain projects.
items.angle: The $33T stablecoin settlement number is no longer a narrative — it is fact. It already surpassed Visa. The $10B in on-chain Treasuries is the next inflection: BlackRock + Ondo + Circle as lead trio is a power concentration worth watching.
items.tweetA: Stablecoins settled $33 trillion in 2025. Visa does about $14 trillion. Nobody noticed the moment the flip happened. 90%+ dollar-denominated. This is not the future of finance — it already happened.
items.tweetB: Tokenized US Treasuries crossed $10B on-chain. BlackRock, Ondo, Circle leading. A dollar savings account for the 4B people who don't have a US brokerage — globally accessible, 24/7, on-chain. That's an export product. The US Treasury market has a new global distribution channel.

items.title: Soviet dissidents were a book club the Politburo overthought — Yurchak's 'performative shift' as a 2026 framework
items.idea: Essay on Nathans's 'To the Success of Our Hopeless Cause' (2024): Soviet dissidents were Muscovite intellectuals defending writer friends. Peak action: 8 people at Red Square, 15 minutes, 1968. Via Yurchak's 'Everything Was Forever': in late-Soviet Russia, citizens were only required to PERFORM loyalty rituals, not believe in them — the 'performative shift' opened space for personal individuation. Author draws explicit 2026 parallels: sclerotic institutions, aging leadership, Twitter as Soviet discourse.
items.angle: The Yurchak insight is the actionable framework for 2026: when institutional language becomes ritualistic, the gap between what words say and what they mean creates freedom. The 'normal person' who follows forms while living their own life is the template for navigating institutions that have lost ideological dynamism.
items.tweetA: The Soviet dissident movement's greatest act was 8 people at Red Square for 15 minutes. The Politburo obsessed over it for years. The ChinaTalk essay on this history is really about 2026: when a regime overthinks a book club, you learn more about the regime than the dissidents.
items.tweetB: Yurchak: late-Soviet citizens only had to PERFORM loyalty, not hold it in their souls. The state stopped probing souls and started requiring rituals. That opened space for personal freedom inside a collapsing system. The essay applies this to 2026. I keep coming back to this framing.

items.title: Brezhnev's political genius was material prosperity, not repression — 'washing machines as wedding dowry' kept dissent small
items.idea: Brezhnev's first project was consumer goods: washing machines, refrigerators, cars as wedding dowries. The 1960s-70s were the best time to be Russian in a century — no purges, no war, material improvement. This made true dissent a 'risky bet for unclear gains.' Dissidents asked for civil liberties; everyone else asked for a refrigerator. Any given city's actual dissident movement fit in a kitchen or two. Material conditions kept the movement small more effectively than the gulag.
items.angle: Prosperity is a more powerful political anesthetic than repression. Voter stability correlates with consumption stability, not civil liberty gains. This explains why fiscal stimulus is such a potent political weapon — and why the current US administration prioritizes keeping material conditions stable despite fiscal cost.
items.tweetA: Brezhnev's political genius wasn't the gulag. It was washing machines. He wanted Soviet cars to be wedding dowries. Material prosperity was the most effective anesthetic against dissent the Soviet system ever found. The dissidents asked for civil liberties. Everyone else asked for a refrigerator. The refrigerator won.
items.tweetB: The most durable political control mechanism has never been repression. It's prosperity. When material conditions improve, abstract demands become 'risky bets for unclear gains.' Brezhnev knew this. The entire 20th century knew this. Fiscal stimulus as political strategy is this insight with decimal places.

items.title: Talon Metals Vault Zone: 20.32m at 7.51% NiEq in Minnesota — geologist says mineralization may form a 'pond or lake'
items.idea: Talon Metals (TLO.TO / TLOFF) confirmed: hole 25TK0568 = 20.32 meters at 7.51% NiEq (2.77% Ni, 5.86% Cu, 0.03% Co, 4.03 g/t Pt+Pd, 2.41 g/t Au) at Tamarack, central Minnesota. Chief Exploration Officer Brian Goldner: 'Massive sulfides flow like water when liquid... I always think of this as an underground river... there's no reason to think it doesn't form a pond or a lake.' Channel model suggests current intercepts are feeder structures, not the main accumulation.
items.angle: The 'pond or lake' thesis separates a good drill hole from a discovery. If the Vault Zone channel feeds a pooled MSU accumulation, grade and tonnage could step-change. Minnesota nickel = domestic US critical minerals with FY2026 NDAA political tailwind.
items.tweetA: Talon Metals hit 20.32m at 7.51% NiEq in Minnesota. The exploration chief's take: massive sulfides flow like water. We're drilling the channel. Channels lead to ponds. Ponds lead to lakes. If the geology is right, the current drill holes are the feeder — not the accumulation. $TLO
items.tweetB: High-grade nickel in Minnesota — not Canada, not Indonesia, not Russia. Domestic US critical minerals at 7.51% NiEq. If the Vault Zone's channel model leads to a pooled accumulation, this is a different scale story. The FY2026 NDAA makes domestic nickel a strategic procurement decision. $TLO
